name: Sequim Lavender Trail
highlights: A cluster of family-owned lavender farms in Sequim's unique rain shadow microclimate
U-pick at peak bloom mid-July — the Sequim Lavender Festival is held the third weekend of July
The contrast between lavender fields and the Olympic Mountains behind them is genuinely striking
Farms include Purple Haze, Graysmarsh, and several others; most offer u-pick and products
hours: Peak season June–August; u-pick mid-July; check individual farm hours
admission: free to visit; u-pick by weight

name: Dungeness Spit — National Wildlife Refuge
highlights: The longest natural sand spit in the United States — 5.5 miles of undeveloped shoreline
Over 250 bird species including black brant, dunlin, and western grebe in migration
New Dungeness Lighthouse at the tip — staffed by volunteer lighthouse keepers
A 10-mile round-trip walk; serene, wind-swept, elemental
hours: Daily sunrise to sunset; small entry fee

name: Makah Museum & Cape Flattery
location: Neah Bay (Makah Reservation — northwest tip of Olympic Peninsula)
highlights: Makah Cultural and Research Center houses artifacts from the Ozette archaeological excavation — a 500-year-old Makah village preserved under a mudslide
Cape Flattery is the northwesternmost point in the contiguous United States — a Makah Tribe permit ($10/vehicle) is required
The 0.75-mile Cape Flattery trail ends at a sea stack viewpoint overlooking Tatoosh Island
Two of the most profound Sacred Terrain experiences on the peninsula, 10 minutes apart
hours: Museum: Wed–Mon 10am–5pm; Cape Flattery: permit required ($10/vehicle) from Makah Tribe

name: Elwha River Restoration
highlights: The largest dam removal project in US history — two dams removed 2011–2014
Led by the Lower Elwha Klallam Tribe to restore salmon runs blocked for 100 years
The Elwha now runs free to the Strait of Juan de Fuca for the first time since 1913
Visitor pullouts along Highway 112 show the recovering river; interpretive signs tell the full story
hours: Open access at all times; Elwha Klallam Heritage Center in Port Angeles has additional exhibits

name: Jamestown Sʻklallam Tribe — 7 Cedars Resort
highlights: The Jamestown S'klallam Tribe is the first in Washington to be entirely self-governing — 7 Cedars Casino and Resort funds cultural programs, language revitalization, and natural resource management
The House of Seven Brothers totem pole at the resort entrance was carved by master carver Dale Faulstich in collaboration with tribal elders
Annual events include the Tribal Journey Canoe Landing (July/August) — when canoe families from tribes across the Pacific Northwest arrive by traditional canoe
The Jamestown S'klallam Tribal Library and Cultural Center in Sequim hosts rotating exhibits and is open to visitors — call ahead
hours: Resort open daily; cultural programming varies

name: Port Townsend — Victorian Seaport
highlights: One of the most architecturally intact Victorian seaports in the US — largely unchanged since the 1890s
Independent bookstores, galleries, and working studios throughout the historic downtown
Olympic Music Festival performs in a barn in the surrounding hills July–September
The slow-travel alternative to rushing the park — a full day spent here rewards
hours: Open access; shops and galleries vary

name: Quileute Nation — La Push
location: La Push, WA — Quileute Reservation, western Olympic Peninsula coast
highlights: La Push sits on the Quileute Reservation — the Quileute people have lived on this coast for thousands of years, and their relationship with the ocean, rivers, and salmon defines the place
Quileute Days (mid-July) is the annual celebration: traditional salmon bake, canoe races, songs, dances, and storytelling. Open to visitors.
The Quileute Oceanside Resort is tribally owned — staying or dining there directly supports the community. First Beach is on Quileute land.
Treat La Push as a visit to someone's home. The Quileute didn't invite the tourists; they're sharing their coast. Respect the land, the signs, and the people.
bookingWindow: Quileute Days in mid-July — no reservation needed for the public celebration. Resort at quileuteoceanside.com.
hours: La Push accessible year-round. Quileute Days mid-July.

name: Centrum — Fort Worden
location: Fort Worden State Park, Port Townsend — former military fort turned arts campus
highlights: Arts residency and performance center in a former coastal artillery fort — jazz, chamber music, fiddle, and writers' workshops on a bluff above the Strait of Juan de Fuca
The Festival of American Fiddle Tunes (July) and the Port Townsend Acoustic Blues Festival (August) are nationally significant — musicians come from across the country
Fort Worden itself is worth the visit: bunkers, lighthouse, officers' quarters, and the Marine Science Center. The setting gives the music a gravity that indoor venues can't.
Port Townsend is 2 hours from the Hoh — a natural first or last night on the peninsula. The town's Victorian architecture and bookstores are their own experience.
bookingWindow: Concerts and festivals at centrum.org. Some are free, some ticketed ($15-$40).
hours: Events year-round; summer festival season Jun-Sep
